texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lithium and boron are trace elements usually found in geothermal waters [17]. Both are very important elements because of their various industrial applications. Lithium based compounds have been used in glass, ceramics, carbon dioxide absorbent systems, Li ion based batteries and antidepressant agent production. Lithium hydroxide constitutes a raw material in the process for various types of industries. During the industrial production process, the need of lowering melting points and reducing coe ffi cient of thermal expansion may be achieved by an addition of lithium [11]. Boron is also one of the useful elements for di ff erent type of industries. Boron is especially used in glass, ceramics, detergents and semiconductors manufacturing industries. Therefore, recovering such important elements from their natural sources is one of the strategies to face the demand for lithium and boron that has increased in recent decades. Recent literature has reported various methods for removal or/and recovery of lithium and boron.
texts.text: Lithium and boron are trace elements usually found in geothermal waters [17]. Both are very important elements because of their various industrial applications. Lithium based compounds have been used in glass, ceramics, carbon dioxide absorbent systems, Li ion based batteries and antidepressant agent production. Lithium hydroxide constitutes a raw material in the process for various types of industries. During the industrial production process, the need of lowering melting points and reducing coe ffi cient of thermal expansion may be achieved by an addition of lithium [11]. Boron is also one of the useful elements for di ff erent type of industries. Boron is especially used in glass, ceramics, detergents and semiconductors manufacturing industries. Therefore, recovering such important elements from their natural sources is one of the strategies to face the demand for lithium and boron that has increased in recent decades. Recent literature has reported various methods for removal or/and recovery of lithium and boron.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Electrodialysis (ED) combined with bipolar membrane, a new system known as bipolar membrane electrodialysis (BMED) has been developed to separate ions from aqueous saline solutions and recover them in their corresponding acids and bases. In this study, separation and recovery of lithium and boron from aqueous solution by BMED method was investigated. Lithium and boron were recovered as LiOH and H3BO3 with BMED, respectively. The in fl uence of process conditions such as applied potential, initial sample volume and pH on BMED performance was monitored. The performance of BMED method increased with an increase in applied voltage but decreased with an increase in initial sample volume. At optimum conditions of 15 V and 0.5 L as initial sample volume, separation and recovery of lithium were 99.6% and 88.3%, respectively, while the respective values for boron were 72.3% and 70.8%. An increase in pH improved separation and recovery of boron more than those of lithium. At pH 12.25, separation and recovery of boron were 95.6% and 78.8%, respectively. The BMED method was found to be e ff ective for simultaneous separation and recovery of lithium and boron from same aqueous solution at optimum operating conditions.
texts.text: Electrodialysis (ED) combined with bipolar membrane, a new system known as bipolar membrane electrodialysis (BMED) has been developed to separate ions from aqueous saline solutions and recover them in their corresponding acids and bases. In this study, separation and recovery of lithium and boron from aqueous solution by BMED method was investigated. Lithium and boron were recovered as LiOH and H3BO3 with BMED, respectively. The in fl uence of process conditions such as applied potential, initial sample volume and pH on BMED performance was monitored. The performance of BMED method increased with an increase in applied voltage but decreased with an increase in initial sample volume. At optimum conditions of 15 V and 0.5 L as initial sample volume, separation and recovery of lithium were 99.6% and 88.3%, respectively, while the respective values for boron were 72.3% and 70.8%. An increase in pH improved separation and recovery of boron more than those of lithium. At pH 12.25, separation and recovery of boron were 95.6% and 78.8%, respectively. The BMED method was found to be e ff ective for simultaneous separation and recovery of lithium and boron from same aqueous solution at optimum operating conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: brane electrodialysis (BMED). Although the separation of lithium from solutions by solvent extraction is possible, the extractants should be highly selective for lithium [2]. The main drawback of solvent extraction is the use of large amount of organic solvent in the whole process, and solvent extraction becomes costly and energy-consuming since recycling the waste solvent for later reuse is very di ffi cult. To escape from the problem of the large amount of contaminated solvents, alternative methods for lithium separation and recovery rather than solvent extraction should be investigated. Parsa et al. [13] has investigated conventional ED process and their results have proven ED process as promising method for lithium recovery from contaminated solution of lithium bromide. Jiang et al., [3] reported the possibility to produce LiOH with a purity of 95% from a solution of Li2CO3 by using electro-electrodialysis bipolar membrane (EEDBM) process. The technology commonly used for the removal of boron from aqueous solutions is the application of chelating resin and seems to be one of the most e ff ective methods [15]. In addition to ion exchange method by chelating resin, membrane processes form another group of preferred technologies for boron removal from water. Recently, adsorption-membrane fi ltration hybrid process was suggested as an alternative method for boron removal from geothermal water [5]. Electrodialysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (ED) was also proposed as an alternative method for boron removal [4,9,16]. Nagasawa et al. [10] reported the possibility to reduce the boron concentration to an acceptable level in solutions of various pH by using BMED. In the study of Nagasawa et al. [10], maximum boron rejection with ED was 45% at pH 9.1 while > 90% of boron rejection was achieved with BMED at pH of 2.1 and 9.1. The weakness of ED for recovery of lithium and boron is due to the di ffi culty of this process to separate lithium and boron from the same stream. However, coupling conventional ED with bipolar membranes forms a BMED process capable to recover lithium and boron separately from the same stream. The BMED method was developed for splitting an aqueous saline solution into its corresponding acid and base without addition of any chemical. This method was reported to be a sustainable and environmentally friendly process [8]. The membranes involved in this process are cation and anion exchange membranes together with bipolar membranes. During the BMED process, positively charged ions migrate towards the cathode through cation exchange membranes and negatively charged ions migrated towards anode passing through anion exchange membranes. A bipolar membrane lets the dissociation of water in the presence of an electrical fi eld. The bipolar membrane is composed of three main parts: an anion-exchange layer, a cationexchange layer, and a hydrophilic interface in the junction of anion and cation-exchange layers. When a direct current is applied to a bipolar membrane in an aqueous solution, the water molecules migrate into the hydrophilic layer where they dissociate into H + and OH -ions [7]. Therefore, a bipolar membrane plays a special role in electromembrane separation process due to its ability of generating proton and hydroxyl ions from water molecules in hydrophilic interface under a direct current passing through it. Consequently, the BMED process yields acid and base as new products in di ff erent compartments owing to the presence of a bipolar membrane. Lithium and boron are then recovered as LiOH and H3BO3 during BMED process, respectively. It is important to notice that the recovery of boron is only possible when the boron in the aqueous solution is in the form of borate ion (B(OH)4 -) instead of boric acid form (H3BO3). The predominance of boron species (B(OH)4 -and H3BO3) is governed by the pH of the solution according to their equilibrium distribution in aqueous solution:

[10] reported the possibility to reduce the boron concentration to an acceptable level in solutions of various pH by using BMED. In the study of Nagasawa et al. [10], maximum boron rejection with ED was 45% at pH 9.1 while > 90% of boron rejection was achieved with BMED at pH of 2.1 and 9.1. The weakness of ED for recovery of lithium and boron is due to the di ffi culty of this process to separate lithium and boron from the same stream. However, coupling conventional ED with bipolar membranes forms a BMED process capable to recover lithium and boron separately from the same stream. The BMED method was developed for splitting an aqueous saline solution into its corresponding acid and base without addition of any chemical. This method was reported to be a sustainable and environmentally friendly process [8]. The membranes involved in this process are cation and anion exchange membranes together with bipolar membranes. During the BMED process, positively charged ions migrate towards the cathode through cation exchange membranes and negatively charged ions migrated towards anode passing through anion exchange membranes. A bipolar membrane lets the dissociation of water in the presence of an electrical fi eld. The bipolar membrane is composed of three main parts: an anion-exchange layer, a cationexchange layer, and a hydrophilic interface in the junction of anion and cation-exchange layers. When a direct current is applied to a bipolar membrane in an aqueous solution, the water molecules migrate into the hydrophilic layer where they dissociate into H + and OH -ions [7]. Therefore, a bipolar membrane plays a special role in electromembrane separation process due to its ability of generating proton and hydroxyl ions from water molecules in hydrophilic interface under a direct current passing through it. Consequently, the BMED process yields acid and base as new products in di ff erent compartments owing to the presence of a bipolar membrane. Lithium and boron are then recovered as LiOH and H3BO3 during BMED process, respectively. It is important to notice that the recovery of boron is only possible when the boron in the aqueous solution is in the form of borate ion (B(OH)4 -) instead of boric acid form (H3BO3). The predominance of boron species (B(OH)4 -and H3BO3) is governed by the pH of the solution according to their equilibrium distribution in aqueous solution:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since H3BO3 is a weak acid with pKa of around 9.14, alkaline solutions in which B(OH)4 -is predominant form are in favor of separation and recovery of boron with BMED. Borate ion [B(OH)4 -], as one of the two species of total boron in natural surface waters, has been known to form complexes with a variety of common cations contained in natural surface waters [1]. The cations such as Na + , Mg 2+ and Ca 2+ show ion-pairing with borate to form di ff erent complexes. It was estimated that 44% of the borate ions in seawater are represented by NaB(OH)4, MgB(OH)4 + and CaB(OH)4 + complexes [1]. The presence of Mg 2+ and Ca 2+ ions in sample solution treated with BMED
texts.text: Since H3BO3 is a weak acid with pKa of around 9.14, alkaline solutions in which B(OH)4 -is predominant form are in favor of separation and recovery of boron with BMED. Borate ion [B(OH)4 -], as one of the two species of total boron in natural surface waters, has been known to form complexes with a variety of common cations contained in natural surface waters [1]. The cations such as Na + , Mg 2+ and Ca 2+ show ion-pairing with borate to form di ff erent complexes. It was estimated that 44% of the borate ions in seawater are represented by NaB(OH)4, MgB(OH)4 + and CaB(OH)4 + complexes [1]. The presence of Mg 2+ and Ca 2+ ions in sample solution treated with BMED

texts.label: text
texts.orig: will have an impact on the purity of the base product. Therefore, as well as for other membrane based technologies, prior to the implementation of BMED to real natural surface waters, removal of Mg 2+ and Ca 2+ will be required. The removal of Mg 2+ and Ca 2+ ions will not only prevent scaling on membranes but will also improve the purity of the base produced during BMED process. The production of base with BMED from the aqueous solutions containing Mg 2+ and Ca 2+ ions will be contaminated by these ions because of the transport of MgB(OH)4 + and CaB(OH)4 + complexes through cation exchange membranes to the base stream. The selective adsorption and/or ion exchange based processes for lithium and boron recovery do not necessarily need any pretreatment for removal of Mg 2+ and Ca 2+ ions. They need, however, a regeneration step for the elution of lithium and boron from their respective selective adsorbents and/or ion exchange resins. In the literature, the selective processes based on adsorption and ion exchange for lithium and boron recovery from geothermal water have been reported [6,12]. By using combined separation process, lithium borate could be directly produced in dilute acidic solution without other elements. Lithium and boron can be then separated from each other and recovered by applying BMED process to the diluted acid solution. The present study focused on the investigation of the feasibility of BMED process for simultaneous recovery of lithium and boron in addition to their separations from aqueous solution. The process parameters such as applied voltage, sample solution volume and pH a ff ecting BMED process performance are discussed.
texts.text: will have an impact on the purity of the base product. Therefore, as well as for other membrane based technologies, prior to the implementation of BMED to real natural surface waters, removal of Mg 2+ and Ca 2+ will be required. The removal of Mg 2+ and Ca 2+ ions will not only prevent scaling on membranes but will also improve the purity of the base produced during BMED process. The production of base with BMED from the aqueous solutions containing Mg 2+ and Ca 2+ ions will be contaminated by these ions because of the transport of MgB(OH)4 + and CaB(OH)4 + complexes through cation exchange membranes to the base stream. The selective adsorption and/or ion exchange based processes for lithium and boron recovery do not necessarily need any pretreatment for removal of Mg 2+ and Ca 2+ ions. They need, however, a regeneration step for the elution of lithium and boron from their respective selective adsorbents and/or ion exchange resins. In the literature, the selective processes based on adsorption and ion exchange for lithium and boron recovery from geothermal water have been reported [6,12]. By using combined separation process, lithium borate could be directly produced in dilute acidic solution without other elements. Lithium and boron can be then separated from each other and recovered by applying BMED process to the diluted acid solution. The present study focused on the investigation of the feasibility of BMED process for simultaneous recovery of lithium and boron in addition to their separations from aqueous solution. The process parameters such as applied voltage, sample solution volume and pH a ff ecting BMED process performance are discussed.

texts.orig: 2. Material and methods
texts.text: 2. Material and methods

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. BMED set up
texts.text: 2.1. BMED set up

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The BMED experiments were carried out using a lab-scale PCCell ED 640 04 model ED cell unit with 4 chambers system. The ED stack is completely assembled with 10 three chamber units (standard cation exchange membranes PC SK, bipolar membranes and anion exchange membrane PC Acid 60) and electrodes (anode: Pt/Ir-MMO coated Tistretched metal, cathode: stainless steel). The sizes of the cell used are 110 × 110 mm and the active area of each membrane is 64 cm 2 . The con fi guration of the membranes in BMED stack was BM-CM-AM-BM for single unit. The end membranes on both cathode and anode side after 10 units formation are cation exchange membranes. In sample compartment, an aqueous solution of Li2B4O7.5H2O (ca. 850 mg-B/L and 250 mg-Li/L) was used. Acid and base compartment contained 500 mL of HCl (3.0 mmol/L) and NaOH (3.0 mmol/L), respectively. The electrode solution was 1 L of NaOH solution (0.1 mol/L). The applied electrical potentials were 10, 13, 15 and 18 V. The fl ow diagram of lithium and boron separations, their recoveries is shown on Fig. 1. The concentration of lithium was measured with an atomic absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu AA-7000 Model). The pH measurements were performed by a pH meter (Hach Lange HQ 40d

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Results and discussion
texts.text: 3. Results and discussion

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Effect of applied electrical potential on lithium and boron separations and recoveries
texts.text: 3.1. Effect of applied electrical potential on lithium and boron separations and recoveries

texts.label: text
texts.orig: E ff ect of applied electrical potential on the separations and recoveries of lithium and boron was investigated using 1.0 L of sample solution containing around 876 ± 63 mg-B/L and 248 ± 35 mg-Li/L. Four di ff erent electrical voltages (10, 13, 15 and 18 V) were tested for experiments. In terms of separations, lithium was separated by 76.6%, 98.0%, 99.9% and 99.9% of e ffi ciencies at 10, 13, 15 and 18 V, respectively while separation e ffi ciencies for boron were 61.6%, 73.5%, 74.5% and 74.7% at respective voltages. The recovery values for lithium and boron were shown in Figs. 2 and 3. While the lithium recoveries in base compartment were 44.3% (10 V), 70.0% (13 V), 83.7% (15 V) and 72.2% (18 V), those of boron were 29.2% (10 V), 60.2% (13 V), 63.7% (15 V) and 52.9% (18 V). Higher lithium separations and recoveries were obtained compared to the respective values for boron. On the other hand, lithium and boron recovery e ffi ciencies were lower than their respective separation e ffi ciencies. In the present study, lithium recovery was lower than its separation e ffi ciency mainly because of the system con fi guration where the end membranes are cation exchange membranes. This means that a part of lithium was transferred to the electrode solution side. The pollutants adsorption onto the membranes and membranes selectivity towards counter ions were considered as common sources of the di ff erence for both lithium and boron separation and recovery e ffi ciencies. That is, anion and cation exchange membranes do not block completely cations and anions, respectively. The lower selectivity of the membranes towards counter ions leads to the phenomenon known as ion leakage. At 15 V, the existence of boron found in the base compartment was 2.41% and the amount of lithium in acid compartment was 2.65% as a result of boron and lithium leakages.
texts.text: E ff ect of applied electrical potential on the separations and recoveries of lithium and boron was investigated using 1.0 L of sample solution containing around 876 ± 63 mg-B/L and 248 ± 35 mg-Li/L. Four di ff erent electrical voltages (10, 13, 15 and 18 V) were tested for experiments. In terms of separations, lithium was separated by 76.6%, 98.0%, 99.9% and 99.9% of e ffi ciencies at 10, 13, 15 and 18 V, respectively while separation e ffi ciencies for boron were 61.6%, 73.5%, 74.5% and 74.7% at respective voltages. The recovery values for lithium and boron were shown in Figs. 2 and 3. While the lithium recoveries in base compartment were 44.3% (10 V), 70.0% (13 V), 83.7% (15 V) and 72.2% (18 V), those of boron were 29.2% (10 V), 60.2% (13 V), 63.7% (15 V) and 52.9% (18 V). Higher lithium separations and recoveries were obtained compared to the respective values for boron. On the other hand, lithium and boron recovery e ffi ciencies were lower than their respective separation e ffi ciencies. In the present study, lithium recovery was lower than its separation e ffi ciency mainly because of the system con fi guration where the end membranes are cation exchange membranes. This means that a part of lithium was transferred to the electrode solution side. The pollutants adsorption onto the membranes and membranes selectivity towards counter ions were considered as common sources of the di ff erence for both lithium and boron separation and recovery e ffi ciencies. That is, anion and cation exchange membranes do not block completely cations and anions, respectively. The lower selectivity of the membranes towards counter ions leads to the phenomenon known as ion leakage. At 15 V, the existence of boron found in the base compartment was 2.41% and the amount of lithium in acid compartment was 2.65% as a result of boron and lithium leakages.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Separation and recovery e ffi ciencies of lithium and boron by BMED process were increased with an increase in electrical potential applied. However, while no di ff erence was obtained in terms of lithium and boron separation e ffi ciencies at 15 V and 18 V, better recovery e ffi ciencies (Figs. 2 and 3) were obtained at 15 V than at 18 V for lithium and boron. As shown in Figs. 2 and 3, the kinetic of lithium and boron increased with applied potential in the fi rst hour of the experiment. After 60 min for lithium and 40 min for boron, a higher recovery was obtained at 15 V than at 18 V. This phenomenon was probably due to early back di ff usion of the lithium and boron at higher electrical potential than the optimum. From these results, 15 V was found to be a suitable potential to achieve good e ffi ciencies for lithium and boron recoveries by BMED system employed. It is important to notice that lithium separation and recovery rates were higher than those of boron. The reason behind this di ff erence was thought to be pH decrease observed in the sample solution (Fig. 4) due probably to the proton leakage from acid compartment through anion exchange membrane. Since the presence of boron species in aqueous solution depends on the pH (Eq. (1)), the decrease in pH of the sample let B(OH)4 -species (ionic form to be transported by electrical potential) convert in H3BO3 in molecular form. Species in molecular form are not transported by electrical potential. Therefore, the separation and recovery of boron by BMED were limited by proton leakage from acid compartment to sample compartment which converted ionic form of boron (B(OH)4 -) in its molecular form (H3BO3).
texts.text: Separation and recovery e ffi ciencies of lithium and boron by BMED process were increased with an increase in electrical potential applied. However, while no di ff erence was obtained in terms of lithium and boron separation e ffi ciencies at 15 V and 18 V, better recovery e ffi ciencies (Figs. 2 and 3) were obtained at 15 V than at 18 V for lithium and boron. As shown in Figs. 2 and 3, the kinetic of lithium and boron increased with applied potential in the fi rst hour of the experiment. After 60 min for lithium and 40 min for boron, a higher recovery was obtained at 15 V than at 18 V. This phenomenon was probably due to early back di ff usion of the lithium and boron at higher electrical potential than the optimum. From these results, 15 V was found to be a suitable potential to achieve good e ffi ciencies for lithium and boron recoveries by BMED system employed. It is important to notice that lithium separation and recovery rates were higher than those of boron. The reason behind this di ff erence was thought to be pH decrease observed in the sample solution (Fig. 4) due probably to the proton leakage from acid compartment through anion exchange membrane. Since the presence of boron species in aqueous solution depends on the pH (Eq. (1)), the decrease in pH of the sample let B(OH)4 -species (ionic form to be transported by electrical potential) convert in H3BO3 in molecular form. Species in molecular form are not transported by electrical potential. Therefore, the separation and recovery of boron by BMED were limited by proton leakage from acid compartment to sample compartment which converted ionic form of boron (B(OH)4 -) in its molecular form (H3BO3).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2.0 L) of sample solution were investigated at 15 V of applied potential. Obtained results showed that there is no e ff ect of initial sample volume on separation performance of BMED. Lithium was separated with a similar performance of 98.6 ± 1.6% at each sample volume while boron separations were 73.2 ± 1.6% at di ff erent initial sample volumes. However, the time required to achieve similar performance was di ff erent. Higher volumes required longer time for reaching a high separation e ffi ciency than lower volumes. Another aspect observed from these results is that lithium was rejected more than boron. Recoveries of lithium and boron in terms of moles numbers increased with an increase in sample volume. However, in terms of lithium and boron recovery e ffi ciency, the performance decreased with an increase in sample volume. The recovery e ffi ciencies of lithium achieved were 88.4% (0.5 L), 83.7% (1.0 L), 79.9% (1.5 L) and 70.9% (2.0 L), respectively (Fig. 5). Similarly, the recovery e ffi ciencies of boron deceased with an increase in sample volume. The recoveries of boron were 70.8% (0.5 L), 63.7% (1.0 L), 54.8% (1.5 L) and 51.9% (2.0 L), respectively, (Fig. 6). However, as same as separation, recovery performances were seen to be strongly related with the time (Figs. 5 and 6). Separation and recovery of boron were reached within 1 h in the case of 0.5 L of sample solution, while higher sample volumes required 2 h to achieve the maximum recovery.
texts.text: 2.0 L) of sample solution were investigated at 15 V of applied potential. Obtained results showed that there is no e ff ect of initial sample volume on separation performance of BMED. Lithium was separated with a similar performance of 98.6 ± 1.6% at each sample volume while boron separations were 73.2 ± 1.6% at di ff erent initial sample volumes. However, the time required to achieve similar performance was di ff erent. Higher volumes required longer time for reaching a high separation e ffi ciency than lower volumes. Another aspect observed from these results is that lithium was rejected more than boron. Recoveries of lithium and boron in terms of moles numbers increased with an increase in sample volume. However, in terms of lithium and boron recovery e ffi ciency, the performance decreased with an increase in sample volume. The recovery e ffi ciencies of lithium achieved were 88.4% (0.5 L), 83.7% (1.0 L), 79.9% (1.5 L) and 70.9% (2.0 L), respectively (Fig. 5). Similarly, the recovery e ffi ciencies of boron deceased with an increase in sample volume. The recoveries of boron were 70.8% (0.5 L), 63.7% (1.0 L), 54.8% (1.5 L) and 51.9% (2.0 L), respectively, (Fig. 6). However, as same as separation, recovery performances were seen to be strongly related with the time (Figs. 5 and 6). Separation and recovery of boron were reached within 1 h in the case of 0.5 L of sample solution, while higher sample volumes required 2 h to achieve the maximum recovery.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An increase in initial volume sample solution of same concentration means an increase in mole numbers of the solution. An increase of moles in the sample results in an increase in molar fl ux of ions in BMED process. This does not mean, however, that recovery e ffi ciency will also increase. Although lithium and boron separation e ffi ciencies were similar at di ff erent sample volumes, their respective recovery e ffi ciencies decreased, however, with an increase in sample volumes (Table 2). The higher was the number of moles (higher volume) of lithium or boron in the solution; more di ffi cult was to achieve a proportional ion recovery. Another aspect observed was that lithium separation and recovery e ffi ciencies were higher than those of boron. The higher separation and recovery of lithium than those of boron were thought to be caused by the decrease of pH in the sample during the experiment as explained in Section 3.1.
texts.text: An increase in initial volume sample solution of same concentration means an increase in mole numbers of the solution. An increase of moles in the sample results in an increase in molar fl ux of ions in BMED process. This does not mean, however, that recovery e ffi ciency will also increase. Although lithium and boron separation e ffi ciencies were similar at di ff erent sample volumes, their respective recovery e ffi ciencies decreased, however, with an increase in sample volumes (Table 2). The higher was the number of moles (higher volume) of lithium or boron in the solution; more di ffi cult was to achieve a proportional ion recovery. Another aspect observed was that lithium separation and recovery e ffi ciencies were higher than those of boron. The higher separation and recovery of lithium than those of boron were thought to be caused by the decrease of pH in the sample during the experiment as explained in Section 3.1.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Effect of initial sample pH on lithium and boron separations and recoveries
texts.text: 3.3. Effect of initial sample pH on lithium and boron separations and recoveries

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Investigation of pH e ff ect on lithium and boron separations and recoveries was achieved at pH 9.25, 10.25 and 12.25 as initial sample pH at 15 V of applied electrical potential. The adjustment of sample pH was made by dissolving corresponding amount of LiOH·H2O in 1.0 L of sample solution containing 827 ± 20.7 mg-B/L. The concentrations of lithium at di ff erent initial sample pH were 218, 385, and 691 mg-Li/L at 9.25, 10.25 and 12.25 as pH, respectively. The results showed that separation e ffi ciencies for lithium were similar at di ff erent pHs investigated with an average rejection of 99.6 ± 0.1%. Alternatively, separation e ffi ciencies of boron increased with an increase in sample pH. The separation e ffi ciencies of boron were 74.5% (pH 9.25), 90.5% (pH 10.25) and 95.6% (pH 12.25) (Table 2). In whole pH regions, while a small decrease in recovery e ffi ciency of lithium was observed as shown in Fig. 7, recoveries of boron, increased as 63.7% (pH 9.25), 71.7% (pH 10.25) and 78.8% (pH 12.25) as shown in Fig. 8. The separation e ffi ciency and recovery of lithium were limited by higher initial lithium concentrations, while those of boron were retarded and limited by higher concentration of hydroxyl ions used to adjust the pH. At pH 12.5, the fi rst 40 min was characterized by lower recovery e ffi ciency of boron (Fig. 8). As hydroxyl ions are counter ions for borate ions, PC Acid 60 anion exchange membrane showed a higher selectivity to hydroxyl ions than borates. PC Acid 60 preference towards hydroxyl ions retards or limits the recovery of boron by time (Fig. 8).
texts.text: Investigation of pH e ff ect on lithium and boron separations and recoveries was achieved at pH 9.25, 10.25 and 12.25 as initial sample pH at 15 V of applied electrical potential. The adjustment of sample pH was made by dissolving corresponding amount of LiOH·H2O in 1.0 L of sample solution containing 827 ± 20.7 mg-B/L. The concentrations of lithium at di ff erent initial sample pH were 218, 385, and 691 mg-Li/L at 9.25, 10.25 and 12.25 as pH, respectively. The results showed that separation e ffi ciencies for lithium were similar at di ff erent pHs investigated with an average rejection of 99.6 ± 0.1%. Alternatively, separation e ffi ciencies of boron increased with an increase in sample pH. The separation e ffi ciencies of boron were 74.5% (pH 9.25), 90.5% (pH 10.25) and 95.6% (pH 12.25) (Table 2). In whole pH regions, while a small decrease in recovery e ffi ciency of lithium was observed as shown in Fig. 7, recoveries of boron, increased as 63.7% (pH 9.25), 71.7% (pH 10.25) and 78.8% (pH 12.25) as shown in Fig. 8. The separation e ffi ciency and recovery of lithium were limited by higher initial lithium concentrations, while those of boron were retarded and limited by higher concentration of hydroxyl ions used to adjust the pH. At pH 12.5, the fi rst 40 min was characterized by lower recovery e ffi ciency of boron (Fig. 8). As hydroxyl ions are counter ions for borate ions, PC Acid 60 anion exchange membrane showed a higher selectivity to hydroxyl ions than borates. PC Acid 60 preference towards hydroxyl ions retards or limits the recovery of boron by time (Fig. 8).
